  "The time complexity of Bucket sort is Θ(n).",
  "Shell sort is a generalization of the Insertion sort.",
  "Shell sort compares elements that are distant apart rather than adjacent.",
  "In Shell sort, the gap value is reduced in each pass until the last pass, where the gap is 1.",
  "In the last pass, Shell sort performs like an insertion sort.",

json_data: WIA2005 Algorithm Design and Analysis Lecture 3 covers Sorting Algorithms.
Dr. Asmiza A. Sani is the lecturer for this session.
The lecture is for Semester 2, Session 2025/26.
The learning objectives include Bubble sort, Counting sort, Radix sort, Bucket sort, and Shell sort.
Sorting refers to arranging data in a particular format.
Sorting algorithms contain a set of instructions to arrange data into a particular order.
Input data for sorting may be stored in an array or list.
Common sort operations are performed in numerical or lexicographical order.
Sorting is considered a fundamental problem in the study of algorithms.
Applications sometimes inherently need to sort information.
Sorting allows for optimized searching of data.
Sorting allows data to be presented in a more readable format.
Sorting algorithms can be classified as In-place vs. Not-In-Place, Stable vs. Not Stable, Adaptive vs. Non-Adaptive, and Online vs. Offline.
Not-in-place sorting algorithms require additional space for comparing or temporary storage of data.
Merge sort is an example of a not-in-place sorting algorithm.
In-place sorting algorithms arrange data without any additional storage.
Insertion sort is an example of an in-place sorting algorithm.
A stable sorting algorithm preserves the sequence of input after the sort has been performed.
If the sequence of input is not preserved after sorting, it is called a not-stable sorting algorithm.
Stability in sorting matters when maintaining the sequence of original input, such as tuples.
An adaptive sorting algorithm can take advantage of a pre-sorted input.
Non-adaptive sorting algorithms do not take advantage of a pre-sorted input.
Insertion Sort is an example of an adaptive sorting algorithm.
An online sorting algorithm can process data as it is being fed and does not require the whole input initially.
Insertion sort is an example of an online algorithm.
An offline sorting algorithm needs all input to be available before it can start processing the output.
Selection sort is an example of an offline algorithm.
Increasing order is any sequence where each element is greater than the previous element.
Non-decreasing order is any sequence where each element is greater than or equal to the previous element.
Decreasing order is any sequence where each element is smaller than the previous element.
Non-increasing order is any sequence where each element is smaller than or equal to the previous element.
Choosing the most appropriate sorting algorithm requires knowing the input data characteristics.
Bubble sort is a popular but inefficient sorting algorithm.
Bubble sort works by repeatedly swapping adjacent elements that are out of order.
The time complexity for Bubble sort is Θ(n²).
Counting sort algorithm sorts elements based on numeric keys between a specific range.
No comparison is done during Counting sort.
Counting sort is used as a subroutine in other sorting algorithms.
The time complexity for Counting sort is Θ(n+k).
Radix sort is a non-comparative integer sorting algorithm.
Radix sort sorts data by grouping keys by individual digits that share the same significant position and value.
Radix sort uses counting sort (or any stable sorting method) to sort integers by digits, starting from the least significant digit.
In Radix sort, each digit is treated as a key.
The time complexity for Radix sort is Θ(d(n+k)).
Bucket sort algorithm creates buckets and puts elements into them.
Bucket sort uses another sorting algorithm (e.g., Insertion sort) to sort elements within each bucket.
After sorting within buckets, elements are taken out and joined to get the sorted result in Bucket sort.
Bucket sort assumes a uniform distribution of input numbers in the range [0,1).
The time complexity of Bucket sort is Θ(n).
Shell sort is a generalization of the Insertion sort.
Shell sort compares elements that are distant apart rather than adjacent.
In Shell sort, the gap value is reduced in each pass until the last pass, where the gap is 1.
In the last pass, Shell sort performs like an insertion sort.
The time complexity of Shell sort ranges from slightly worse than Θ(n log n) to Θ(n²), depending on the gap sequence.
Dataset 1, `[1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10]`, is nearly sorted data.
Insertion Sort is best suited for nearly sorted data.
Dataset 2, `[100, 90, 80, 70, 60, 50, 40, 30, 20, 10]`, is completely reverse ordered data.
Merge Sort or Heap Sort are best suited for reverse sorted data.
Dataset 3, `[5, 1, 5, 3, 5, 2, 1, 3, 5, 2]`, contains many repeated values.
Counting Sort or Stable Sorts are best suited for data with many duplicates.
Dataset 4, `[42, 7, 89, 3, 105, 64, 32, 19, 91, 58]`, consists of random unsorted integers within a wide range.
Quick Sort and Merge Sort are best suited for random unsorted data with a large range.
Lecture 4 will cover String Matching Algorithms.
"Introduction to Algorithms, 3rd edition" by Thomas H. Cormen et al. is a reference for the lecture.
"Algorithm, 5th Edition" by Robert Sedgewick and Kevin Wayne is a reference for the lecture.
